1: A mighty angel descends wrapped in cloud, with a rainbow above his head, face like the sun, legs like pillars of fire. The description deliberately echoes Christ's appearance in Revelation 1:15-16 (feet like burnished bronze, face like the sun) and the divine throne's rainbow (Revelation 4:3). Many interpreters identify this figure as Christ himself — the pre-incarnate Word appearing in angelic form, as in the OT theophanies (the angel of the LORD in Genesis 16:7-13; Exodus 3:2-6). Whether identified as Christ or as a supreme Christ-type angel, the figure bears the marks of divine-Christological authority.
2: The little scroll lying open in his hand — in contrast to the sealed scroll of Revelation 5 that only the Lamb could open, this scroll is already open. What the Lamb opened (Revelation 5:9) is now disclosed to John for proclamation. The right foot on the sea and left foot on the land signals cosmic lordship: Christ's word and authority extends over the totality of the created order (Matthew 28:18: 'All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me').
3: The voice of the mighty angel 'like the roar of a lion' — Christ is the Lion of the tribe of Judah (Revelation 5:5). The lion's roar is the prophetic call (Amos 3:8: 'The lion has roared — who will not fear? The Sovereign LORD has spoken — who can but prophesy?'). The seven thunders that respond are the fullness of divine speech — seven indicating completeness. The word of Christ, when spoken, fills the cosmos with its response.
4: The seven thunders speak but their words are sealed — not everything is disclosed even in the Christological revelation of Revelation. Christ modeled this: 'I have much more to say to you, more than you can now bear' (John 16:12); 'about that day or hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father' (Matthew 24:36). Divine revelation has its own timetable; what is sealed by divine authority remains sealed until its appointed time.
5: The mighty angel raises his right hand toward heaven — the oath posture of Daniel 12:7, where 'the man clothed in linen' (an OT Christ-type figure) raised his hands and swore an oath by the eternal God. The oath is addressed to the Creator God — even the Christ-type figure swears by the Father, consistent with the Son's economic submission to the Father within the Trinity (John 5:30; 1 Corinthians 15:28).
6: 'There will be no more delay.' The martyrs' cry of 'how long?' (Revelation 6:10) is answered: the time of waiting is ending. Christ promised: 'Yes, I am coming soon' (Revelation 22:20); here the angel announces that the mystery of God will be fulfilled at the seventh trumpet's sound. The Creator God by whom the oath is sworn is the one in whom Christ's authority is grounded — the triune God's commitment to finish what he started in creation and redemption is the ground of the 'no more delay' announcement.
7: 'The mystery of God will be fulfilled, just as he announced to his servants the prophets.' The mystery of God is the gospel — Romans 16:25-26 ('the revelation of the mystery hidden for long ages past, now revealed and made known through the prophetic writings, so that all the Gentiles might believe and obey'); Ephesians 3:4-6 ('the mystery of Christ... that through the gospel the Gentiles are heirs together with Israel'). The seventh trumpet's announcement (Revelation 11:15: 'the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ') is the fulfillment of this mystery.
8: The heavenly voice commands John to take the little scroll from the mighty angel — the prophetic commission requires active appropriation of the word. John is not passively handed revelation; he is sent to take it. This pattern echoes the prophetic calls of Isaiah (Isaiah 6:8-9), Jeremiah (Jeremiah 1:9-10: words put in his mouth), and Ezekiel (Ezekiel 2:8-3:3: the scroll given to eat). Christ commissions his prophets in continuity with the OT prophetic tradition.
9: John is told: 'Take it and eat it. It will make your stomach bitter, but in your mouth it will be sweet as honey.' Ezekiel ate the scroll (Ezekiel 2:8-3:3: 'it tasted as sweet as honey'); Jeremiah found God's words to be his joy and heart's delight (Jeremiah 15:16) but also found them bringing reproach. The sweetness and bitterness of Christ's word is its dual character: the gospel is sweet to those who receive it (eternal life) and bitter in its implications (the judgment of all who reject it).
10: John ate the scroll — sweet in his mouth but bitter in his stomach. The prophetic experience of receiving divine revelation involves both dimensions. Christ himself experienced this bitterness: he took the cup of God's wrath (Mark 14:36: 'take this cup from me') and drank it on behalf of others, so that those who receive his word might ultimately have only sweetness. The bitterness of prophetic proclamation participates in the bitterness of the cross.
11: 'You must prophesy again about many peoples, nations, languages, and kings.' The prophetic recommissioning echoes Jeremiah (Jeremiah 1:10: 'over nations and over kingdoms') and the Great Commission (Matthew 28:18-20: 'all nations'; Acts 1:8: 'to the ends of the earth'). Christ commissions his witnesses to the full breadth of the nations. The recommissioning after the bitter-sweet experience ensures that the difficulty of prophetic ministry does not silence the proclamation.
